title: Urban Planner / City Planner
description: You shape how cities grow — through zoning codes, comprehensive plans, development review, and community engagement — influencing the built environment at the scale of neighborhoods and regions.
personalNote: If a single building has always felt too small for what you want to do, planning is where architects find their ambition at the right scale.

title: Historic Preservation Specialist
description: You protect and rehabilitate the built record of human history — evaluating historic significance, documenting existing conditions, and guiding the sensitive renovation of significant places.
personalNote: Preservation draws people who believe the built past is worth fighting for.

title: Sustainability Consultant
description: You help projects, organizations, and communities reduce their environmental impact — through certification pathways, policy frameworks, design strategy, and stakeholder engagement.
personalNote: Sustainability consulting rewards people who can hold both the technical and the political.

title: Community Development Director
description: You lead organizations focused on housing, economic opportunity, and neighborhood revitalization — managing programs, funding streams, and community relationships to create lasting change.
personalNote: This path is for architects who wanted their work to serve communities, not just clients.

title: Zoning / Land Use Consultant
description: You help property owners, developers, and municipalities navigate the complex regulatory landscape of zoning, land use approvals, variances, and entitlements.
personalNote: If you ever found yourself more interested in the legal and regulatory landscape than the design, this path exists.

title: LEED / WELL Consultant
description: You guide projects through green building certification processes — coordinating documentation, advising on strategy, and helping teams achieve sustainability targets.
personalNote: The best consultants use the certification framework to push projects toward genuine performance — not just paperwork.

title: Architecture Adjunct Faculty
description: You teach design studios, technical courses, or history and theory seminars — sharing your professional experience while engaging with the next generation of practitioners.
personalNote: Adjunct teaching is how most practitioners stay connected to ideas — the intellectual energy it provides is genuinely irreplaceable.

title: Academic Program Coordinator
description: You manage the operational and administrative dimensions of an architecture program — coordinating curriculum, supporting faculty, and ensuring the program runs well behind the scenes.
personalNote: Coordination roles offer stability in the academic environment — the influence is quieter but real.

title: Curriculum Developer (AEC Focus)
description: You design educational programs, courses, and training materials for architecture schools, professional development organizations, or online learning platforms.
personalNote: If you've always thought 'this should be taught differently,' this is the role where you get to actually fix it.

title: University Facilities Architect
description: You manage capital projects and design standards for a university campus — representing the institution's interests while maintaining the campus's architectural character.
personalNote: University facilities roles offer stability, meaningful scale, and the satisfaction of stewarding a coherent built environment.

title: K-12 Educational Design Specialist
description: You design schools and learning environments — specialized buildings with complex programming, tight budgets, and direct impact on how children experience their education.
personalNote: Few building types have more direct human impact than schools.

title: Independent Design Consultant
description: You work on your own terms — advising clients on design decisions, managing small projects, or providing specialized expertise that firms need but don't want to hire full-time.
personalNote: Independent practice rewards discipline and self-direction — the freedom is real, and so is the volatility.

title: AEC Career Coach
description: You help architecture and AEC professionals navigate career transitions, licensing challenges, job searches, and professional development — combining industry expertise with coaching methodology.
personalNote: People who've navigated the profession well — and can communicate what they've learned — are exactly who this field needs.

title: Firm Culture / Succession Consultant
description: You help architecture firms with the human side of business — succession planning, team culture, compensation design, leadership development, and organizational change.
personalNote: This path is for people who've spent enough time inside the profession to understand what's actually broken — and want to help fix it.

title: Architectural Content Creator
description: You produce educational or inspirational content about architecture — videos, essays, courses, social content — building an audience around your expertise and perspective.
personalNote: The architects building the biggest content platforms aren't necessarily the most famous designers — they're the ones who decided their voice mattered and started publishing it.

title: Architecture Podcast / Newsletter Host
description: You build a media platform in the AEC space — interviewing practitioners, publishing analysis, and creating the content that shapes how the profession thinks about itself.
personalNote: The best AEC media is still being built — if you want to shape the conversation and you're willing to build an audience slowly, this is a viable path.

title: Architectural Photographer
description: You document and interpret built work through photography — creating the images that represent buildings to the public, clients, and the profession.
personalNote: Architects who transition to photography bring something most photographers don't have — they understand the building from the inside out.

title: Manufacturer's Rep / Product Specialist
description: You represent building product manufacturers to the architecture and construction industry — educating specifiers, building relationships, and helping projects find the right materials and systems.
personalNote: Manufacturer's reps often out-earn and out-travel architects, with less stress and more human interaction.

title: Construction Technology Sales
description: You sell software and technology tools to the construction and architecture industry — Revit, Procore, construction management platforms, and emerging tech.
personalNote: Tech sales rewards architects who understand the product deeply and can communicate its value to the people they used to work alongside.

title: PropTech / Real Estate Tech
description: You work at the intersection of real estate, technology, and data — building platforms, tools, and services that change how buildings are transacted, managed, and experienced.
personalNote: Architects who understand how buildings actually work are well-positioned for product, consulting, or business development roles in PropTech.

title: Healthcare Facility Planner
description: You specialize in the planning and design of healthcare environments — hospitals, clinics, and care facilities — understanding the complex operational and clinical requirements that govern how these buildings must function.
personalNote: Healthcare design is where the gap between good and mediocre design has direct human consequence.
